Lawrence A. Haun
Roger A. Brown
Lew 0rrtces or
LewnrNcr A. HnuN
El.vco and onc.half South washington Str.a 
-'
Posr OIIicc Box 1056
Sonora, California 953?0
Telcphonc (209) 532-195E Tclccopicr (209) 532-l963lhg i"
S i'r .,,i'$
December 11, 1990
Kevin Braaten-Moen
Techni,cal Assistance consuLtant
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J street, Suj.te 800
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804
RE: conf imati-on of Telephone Advice
Dear Kevin:
This confirms j,nfornal telephone advice you gave me on
December 6, 1990. I appreciate your prompt response to my
questions.
I represent an advertising agency which occasionally
provides services to political 
carnpaigns. My client receives
fees and commissions frorn his client, a carnpaign cornmittee. My
cLientrs contract provides for a standard agency commission on
all advertising created and placed for the carnpaign.
My client retains various independent contractors to help
with the campaign. These subcontractors typically perforrn tasks
which would otherwise be done by the agencyrs regular empfoyees.
These subcontractors rnay provide free lance creative consulting,
media purchasing, or advice on creative or technical matters. 
My
client hrishes to share his cornrnissions with his subcontractors.
Commission splits !ri11 come from my clientrs earned
commissions, not from the campaign comrnittee. As a result, the
campai-gn cornmittee will- receive and report details of gross
amounts they paid for subvendor goods and seryices, rny clientrs
commission, and the fees and conmissions paid to subvendors by
the carnpaign cornmittee.
For example, my client receives a standard l-5% agency
cornmission for television advertising. 
He contracts with a
creative consultant and a media placement consuLtant to help
create and distribute the advertising for broadcast. My client
nay then pay a portion of his 1,5? commission to one or more of
his subcontractors. The carnpaign cornmittee will discl-ose the
fuIl conmission paid to rny client. 
Is ny client requj-red to
report the portion of his own comnission he gave to the
subcontractor?
You advised that ny client is not requj.red to provide his
client, the comnittee, with informatj-on on comrnission splits with
his subcontractors. You also concl-uded that the canpaign


Kevin Braaten-Moen
December 1,1, 1990
Page 2
conmittee is not required to report as subvendor payrnents these
commission spl-its my client rnakes with his subcontractors.
You stated that the Fair Political Practices Commission has
never advised people to report subcontractor commj-ssion splits as
subvendor paynents pursuant to Government code section 84303.
Moreover, the FPPC has gone further and advised people they do
not have to report these palaflents.
You explained that the cornmission has no advice letters,
opinions or other written material-s t/hich address these
questions. Therefore, f $rou1d appreciate written confirrnation of
your advice to me.
My client wants to report everything the law requires, 
He
does not want to overreport where to do so would reveal
proprietary inforrnatj,on and trade secrets. If cornpetitors
discover the amount of my clientrs commission he shares with
subcontractors, they will gain a cornpetitive advantage. If
employees learn that outside consul-tants receive a portion of rny
cl-ientts cornmission, they rnay become disgruntled and quit or
demand raises. 
These are internal, busi-ness matters the
disclosure of which serves no meaningful public purpose.
These commission splits rnay be rnade in other ways as well-.
In sone cases, the agency rnay write a check to the subcontractor.
In other cases, such as with nedia buyers, the subcontractor may
retain the commission split from the gross amount the client pays
for the nedia buy. My client may al,so wait until the end of the
canpaign to distribute cornrnission splits. 
In each example, it is
ny client's money that is split with subcontractors, not the
committee's. Would your advice be any different in any of these
exanpl es ?
I understar:d your advice vras based upon vour examj.nation of
the provisions of Government Code section 84303 and Regulation
18431, your discussion with Carla Wardlow of the Commission
staff, and your research of prior commission advice.
If I have rnisunderstood your advice, please 1et me know as
soon as possible. Others/ise, ny client wiII follow your advice
until further notice,
Thank you again for your pronpt and courteous heIp.
cc: cl ient
RAB:ltE:218.002
Brown
